news_summary: NASA’s CAPSTONE successfully advances lunar exploration by completing extended mission tests of innovative technologies.
news_summary_fr: Le programme CAPSTONE de la NASA fait progresser avec succès l'exploration lunaire en menant à bien des essais dans le cadre d'une mission prolongée portant sur des technologies innovantes.
news_summary_es: El proyecto CAPSTONE de la NASA impulsa con éxito la exploración lunar al completar las pruebas de misión prolongada de tecnologías innovadoras.

articles.title: NASA’s CAPSTONE Completes Extended Mission Testing Lunar Technologies
articles.summary: As NASA prepares for a sustained human presence on the Moon, missions will increasingly require spacecraft that can navigate and communicate without a direct connection to Earth. NASA’s Cislunar Autonomous Positioning System Technology Operations and Navigation Experiment, or CAPSTONE, validated and advanced these capabilities. Designed to test and validate technologies in lunar orbit, CAPSTONE launched […]
articles.content:
As NASA prepares for a sustained human presence on the Moon, missions will increasingly require spacecraft that can navigate and communicate without a direct connection to Earth.

NASA’s Cislunar Autonomous Positioning System Technology Operations and Navigation Experiment, or CAPSTONE, validated and advanced these capabilities.

Designed to test and validate technologies in lunar orbit, CAPSTONE launched in June 2022 and became the first U.S. commercial mission at the Moon. The spacecraft tested operations in three-body orbits around the Moon, using the combined gravity of Earth and the Moon to reduce the fuel needed to maintain a stable lunar path. It became the first spacecraft to fly and characterize this orbit for future exploration and science missions. Owned and operated by Advanced Space, the microwave-sized spacecraft then received a 15-month mission extension, becoming a testbed for advanced communications, networking, autonomous navigation, and software-defined satellite technologies.

Dylan Schmidt, CAPSTONE assembly integration and test lead, right, and Lachlan Moore, systems integration engineer, left, install solar panels onto the CAPSTONE spacecraft at Tyvak Nano-Satellite Systems, Inc., in Irvine, California. NASA/Dominic Hart

Rather than launch a new satellite, NASA’s Research and Technology Mission Directorate demonstrated that CAPSTONE’s existing hardware could host new applications after launch, transforming the spacecraft into a cost-effective, flexible lunar technology demonstration platform. NASA’s SCaN (Space Communications and Navigation) Division will now use the data to demonstrate innovative networking and navigation techniques on future experiments.

“Operating multiple experiments simultaneously aboard the same spacecraft allows NASA to evaluate how these technologies perform together in a real lunar environment,” said Greg Stover, director of the Advanced Research and Technology Division within NASA’s Research and Technology Mission Directorate at NASA Headquarters in Washington. “Investments in autonomous operations and resilient communications infrastructure are essential to ensuring U.S. leadership as activity around the Moon continues to increase.”

Two experiments aboard CAPSTONE used software-defined infrastructure to advance two future mission essentials: autonomous navigation and deep space communications. The autonomous Navigation, Guidance, and Control software, or autoNGC, is designed to allow a spacecraft to determine where it is, where it is going, and how to get where it needs to be without waiting for instructions from the ground. While portions of the software had previously flown in Earth orbit, CAPSTONE marked the first time autoNGC was tested at the Moon.

“To really demonstrate that something works, you have to fly it,” said Sun Hur-Diaz, principal investigator for the autoNGC technology development project at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland. “The real environment is key.”

To really demonstrate that something works you have to fly it. The real environment is key. Sun Hur-Diaz Principal Investigator for the autoNGC Project, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center

Researchers also evaluated how autoNGC performed with limited contact to Earth. While NASA’s Deep Space Network antennas were supporting the Artemis II crewed test flight around the Moon, CAPSTONE’s communications window dropped to just a few passes per week.

Those gaps became one of the experiment’s most valuable tests. Without data from Earth, autoNGC determined CAPSTONE’s location using an onboard star tracker camera to image the Moon, Earth, and other celestial bodies. The camera-based system, known as optical navigation, at times outperformed ground-based methods for real-time onboard navigation, advancing technologies for future deep-space missions.

Alongside autonomous navigation testing, CAPSTONE also tested delay/disruption tolerant networking (DTN), a communications architecture designed for deep space. Unlike Earth-based internet systems, deep space communications must function despite long delays and frequent signal gaps. The DTN system addresses those challenges by storing information on the spacecraft when no connection is available and automatically forwarding it once communications are restored. With these demonstrations, CAPSTONE became the first to fly the latest DTN protocols beyond Earth orbit and the first to run them in NASA’s core Flight System, an open-source framework that can be implemented on any spacecraft.

In one demonstration, engineers began transmitting data from CAPSTONE to Earth, but the connection ended before the transfer was complete. The spacecraft stored the remaining data until the next communications opportunity, and transmission resumed automatically. Every piece of data made it home.

Artist’s rendering depicting astronauts, habitats, rovers, power systems, and cargo operations supporting sustained human activities at the Moon Base near the lunar South Pole. The technologies CAPSTONE tested may be key to NASA’s growing lunar communications and navigation infrastructure. NASA

“You can imagine an astronaut walking behind a lunar hill or descending into a crater and temporarily losing connectivity,” said Ben Anderson, a systems engineer for the Near Space Network at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland. “This technology allows that data to be automatically retransmitted once communications are restored.”

In addition to its primary achievements, CAPSTONE’s second life as a software-defined testing platform demonstrated that new technologies can be affordably tested and proven directly in their operational environment.

After nearly four years of technology maturation, NASA’s activities on CAPSTONE concluded in June 2026, while Advanced Space will continue to use the spacecraft as a technology development testbed.

The CAPSTONE spacecraft was designed and built by Terran Orbital and is owned and operated by Advanced Space. NASA’s Research and Technology Mission Directorate managed the mission through the Small Spacecraft and Distributed Systems program, based at NASA’s Ames Research Center in California’s Silicon Valley. Elements of the CAPSTONE technology suite were supported by NASA’s Small Business Innovation Research program. The autoNGC and DTN demonstrations conducted during CAPSTONE’s extended mission were managed by NASA’s SCaN Division, based at NASA Headquarters in Washington.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on NASA's CAPSTONE mission successfully testing and validating advanced autonomous navigation and deep space communication technologies in lunar orbit. These achievements have significant implications for sustained human presence on the Moon and future deep space exploration, representing a meaningful technological breakthrough with broad impact on space exploration capabilities. The article provides detailed context about the mission, technologies tested, and their importance.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s CAPSTONE mission tests new navigation and communication tech around the Moon
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is preparing for longer human stays on the Moon. To do this, spacecraft will need to navigate and communicate on their own without always relying on signals from Earth.

**What happened** – NASA’s CAPSTONE spacecraft, launched in June 2022, tested new technologies in lunar orbit. It was the first U.S. commercial mission at the Moon and flew in a special orbit that uses gravity from both Earth and the Moon to save fuel. After its main mission, CAPSTONE was given 15 more months to test advanced systems like autonomous navigation, which lets the spacecraft find its position and path without help from Earth, and delay/disruption tolerant networking (DTN), which helps send data even when signals are weak or interrupted.

**Impact** – CAPSTONE showed that spacecraft can operate more independently in space. For example, when communication with Earth was limited, the spacecraft used a star tracker camera to figure out where it was by looking at the Moon and stars. This method sometimes worked better than relying on Earth signals. The DTN system also proved it can store and resend data automatically if the connection drops, which is important for astronauts who might lose contact temporarily behind lunar hills or craters.

**What's next step** – NASA will use what it learned from CAPSTONE to improve future missions. The data will help build better communication and navigation systems for astronauts living and working on the Moon. Meanwhile, the company Advanced Space will keep using CAPSTONE as a testing platform for new space technologies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s CAPSTONE mission successfully tested important new technologies that help spacecraft navigate and communicate on their own around the Moon, paving the way for future lunar exploration.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission CAPSTONE de la NASA teste de nouvelles technologies de navigation et de communication autour de la Lune
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA se prépare à des séjours humains plus longs sur la Lune. Pour cela, les engins spatiaux devront naviguer et communiquer de manière autonome, sans toujours dépendre des signaux venant de la Terre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le satellite CAPSTONE de la NASA, lancé en juin 2022, a testé de nouvelles technologies en orbite lunaire. Il a été la première mission commerciale américaine autour de la Lune et a volé sur une orbite spéciale utilisant la gravité de la Terre et de la Lune pour économiser du carburant. Après sa mission principale, CAPSTONE a bénéficié de 15 mois supplémentaires pour tester des systèmes avancés comme la navigation autonome, qui permet à l’engin de se localiser et de se diriger sans aide terrestre, et le réseau tolérant aux retards et interruptions (DTN), qui aide à transmettre des données même quand les signaux sont faibles ou coupés.

**Impact** – CAPSTONE a démontré que les engins spatiaux peuvent fonctionner de façon plus indépendante dans l’espace. Par exemple, quand la communication avec la Terre était limitée, le satellite a utilisé une caméra pour observer la Lune et les étoiles et ainsi déterminer sa position. Cette méthode a parfois mieux fonctionné que les signaux venant de la Terre. Le système DTN a aussi prouvé qu’il peut stocker et renvoyer automatiquement les données si la connexion est interrompue, ce qui est important pour les astronautes qui pourraient perdre contact temporairement derrière des collines lunaires ou dans des cratères.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA utilisera les enseignements de CAPSTONE pour améliorer les futures missions. Ces données aideront à construire de meilleurs systèmes de communication et de navigation pour les astronautes vivant et travaillant sur la Lune. Pendant ce temps, la société Advanced Space continuera d’utiliser CAPSTONE comme plateforme de test pour de nouvelles technologies spatiales.

**Phrase clé** – La mission CAPSTONE de la NASA a testé avec succès des technologies importantes qui permettent aux engins spatiaux de naviguer et communiquer de manière autonome autour de la Lune, ouvrant la voie à l’exploration lunaire future.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión CAPSTONE de la NASA prueba nuevas tecnologías de navegación y comunicación alrededor de la Luna
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA se está preparando para estancias humanas más largas en la Luna. Para esto, las naves espaciales necesitarán navegar y comunicarse por sí solas sin depender siempre de señales desde la Tierra.

**Qué pasó** – La nave CAPSTONE de la NASA, lanzada en junio de 2022, probó nuevas tecnologías en órbita lunar. Fue la primera misión comercial estadounidense en la Luna y voló en una órbita especial que usa la gravedad combinada de la Tierra y la Luna para ahorrar combustible. Después de su misión principal, CAPSTONE recibió 15 meses más para probar sistemas avanzados como la navegación autónoma, que permite que la nave se ubique y se dirija sin ayuda desde la Tierra, y la red tolerante a retrasos e interrupciones (DTN), que ayuda a enviar datos aunque las señales sean débiles o se corten.

**Impacto** – CAPSTONE demostró que las naves pueden operar de forma más independiente en el espacio. Por ejemplo, cuando la comunicación con la Tierra era limitada, la nave usó una cámara para observar la Luna y las estrellas y así determinar su posición. Este método a veces funcionó mejor que depender de señales desde la Tierra. El sistema DTN también probó que puede guardar y reenviar datos automáticamente si la conexión se pierde, lo cual es importante para los astronautas que podrían perder contacto temporalmente detrás de colinas lunares o en cráteres.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA usará lo aprendido de CAPSTONE para mejorar futuras misiones. Los datos ayudarán a construir mejores sistemas de comunicación y navegación para los astronautas que vivan y trabajen en la Luna. Mientras tanto, la empresa Advanced Space seguirá usando CAPSTONE como plataforma de prueba para nuevas tecnologías espaciales.

**Frase clave** – La misión CAPSTONE de la NASA probó con éxito tecnologías clave que ayudan a las naves a navegar y comunicarse por sí solas alrededor de la Luna, preparando el camino para la exploración lunar futura.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a small, stylized spacecraft orbiting a softly glowing Moon, connected by delicate, glowing threads of light symbolizing autonomous navigation and resilient deep-space communication, with Earth visible in the distant background and subtle celestial bodies guiding its path.
